label: Segment Anything
definition: A promptable foundation model for image segmentation that, given a click, box, or rough region, returns a clean mask for almost any object, in any image, with no fine-tuning. It brought zero-shot generality to segmentation by learning the class-agnostic notion of what counts as an object, trained on a billion masks built by a model-and-annotator data engine.

body.text: Segment Anything, often called SAM, is a foundation model for image segmentation: a single large model trained so broadly that it can cut out almost any object in almost any image on demand. Rather than being trained for a fixed list of categories, it is promptable, the user supplies a prompt, a point clicked on an object, a bounding box drawn around it, or a coarse mask, and the model returns a clean, pixel-accurate mask for the object the prompt indicates. Released by Meta in 2023, it reframed segmentation from a closed-set classification problem into an interactive, open-ended one, and it can run the prompting automatically across a grid of points to segment every object in an image at once.

It matters because it brought to segmentation the kind of zero-shot generality that large pretrained models had already brought to language and image-text matching. Conventional segmentation models, however strong, are bound to the categories in their training labels and degrade on anything unfamiliar; SAM instead learns the general notion of what counts as an object and where its boundary lies, so it transfers to medical scans, satellite imagery, microscopy, and everyday photos without retraining. That makes it a reusable building block: downstream systems prompt SAM to produce masks and then attach their own labels or logic, turning a hard perception step into a solved upstream service much as a pretrained backbone is reused across tasks.

Three pieces make this work. A heavy image encoder, a vision transformer pretrained in the style of a masked autoencoder, runs once per image to produce a rich feature embedding; a lightweight prompt encoder turns the click, box, or mask into a compact representation; and a fast decoder fuses the two to emit masks in milliseconds, so a user can prompt the same expensive image embedding interactively many times. Because a single point is often ambiguous, a click on a person's shirt could mean the shirt, the torso, or the whole person, the model returns several nested mask candidates with confidence scores and lets the application pick. The training data is the other half of the story: SAM was trained on a billion masks built by a data engine in which the model and human annotators bootstrapped each other, the model proposing masks and annotators correcting them, a loop that grew the dataset far beyond what manual labeling alone could reach.

Segment Anything sits at the convergence of the segmentation tradition and the foundation-model paradigm. Its masks are class-agnostic, which complements rather than replaces semantic segmentation and instance segmentation: those tasks name and group regions, while SAM excels at producing the precise region boundary for whatever a prompt points at. In practice it is frequently paired with an object detection or text-grounding model that supplies prompts automatically, so a text query or detected box drives SAM to return the corresponding mask, yielding open-vocabulary segmentation from the combination.

image.alt: The Segment Anything Model architecture: an input image passes through a heavy image encoder into an image embedding, a lightweight prompt encoder turns a mask, points, a box, or text into prompt embeddings, and a fast mask decoder combines the two to output several valid object masks each with a confidence score.
image.caption: Segment Anything is a promptable segmentation model. A heavy image encoder runs once to embed the picture, then a lightweight prompt encoder and mask decoder turn a click, box, or rough region into a valid object mask in real time, generalizing to objects it never saw in training.
image.credit: Meta AI Research (Kirillov et al., Segment Anything)
